title: Psychology
intro: Psychology questions in this unit cluster heavily around a small set of towering figures and the schools they founded. Sigmund Freud and Jean Piaget are the two most-asked answers by a wide margin, reflecting the canon's emphasis on psychoanalysis and stage-based developmental theory; Daniel Kahneman, Albert Bandura, and B.F. Skinner (asked under both his full name and initials) round out the most frequently cited individual thinkers. Alongside named thinkers, a large share of clues target general topics and processes rather than people — memory, emotions, personality, attention, and stress recur across many different questions as connective or common-link material rather than as answers tied to a single researcher.

The canon draws most heavily on twentieth-century American and European experimental and clinical psychology. Landmark studies are asked both for their designers and as standalone answerlines: Milgram's obedience studies, Zimbardo's Stanford Prison Experiment, Asch's conformity line-judging task, Sherif's Robbers Cave Experiment, and Festinger's cognitive dissonance work (including the doomsday-cult study written up in When Prophecy Fails) form the backbone of the social psychology material. Developmental questions center on Piaget's stages, Kohlberg's moral dilemmas, Erikson's psychosocial stages, and attachment research built on Harlow's monkey studies, while behaviorist material runs from Pavlovian and Thorndike-style animal conditioning through Watson and Skinner to Bandura's Bobo doll experiment on observational learning.

Clinical and abnormal psychology is well represented through named disorders (schizophrenia, depression, PTSD, OCD, bipolar disorder, autism spectrum disorder, borderline personality disorder) and the diagnostic apparatus of the DSM, alongside therapeutic figures such as Aaron Beck and Albert Ellis for cognitive-behavioral approaches. Humanistic and positive psychology appear through Maslow, Carl Rogers, Martin Seligman, and Viktor Frankl, while personality and testing questions draw on trait theory, factor analysis, and instruments like the Rorschach and Thematic Apperception Test. A smaller but consistent thread of cognitive science questions covers memory research (Ebbinghaus, Loftus), perceptual and Gestalt phenomena (Wertheimer, Kohler, the phi phenomenon), and judgment and decision-making under Kahneman's heuristics-and-biases program.

sections.name: Founding Schools: Structuralism & Functionalism
sections.blurb: The late-19th-century origins of experimental psychology in Wundt's introspective structuralism and James's pragmatist functionalism.

entries.note: American founder of functionalism who wrote The Principles of Psychology and theorized the James-Lange theory of emotion
Method of self-examination of one's own mental states, central to Wundt and Titchener's structuralism
Subject of William James's "stream of consciousness" concept and early debates over introspective study of awareness
German psychologist who founded the first experimental psychology laboratory in 1879 and pioneered structuralism
American psychologist, first president of the APA, and founder of developmental/child psychology and adolescence studies
American philosopher-psychologist (b. 1859), a founder of functionalism and pragmatism, influential in progressive education

sections.name: Gestalt Psychology & Perception
sections.blurb: The German school studying how the mind organizes sensory wholes, alongside classic perceptual phenomena and illusions.

entries.note: Early-20th-century German school holding that the mind organizes perception into wholes greater than the sum of their parts
German for "shape/form," the school emphasizing perceptual organization into unified wholes
Visual phenomena where perception diverges from physical reality, often explained by Gestalt principles
Illusion of motion from static images shown in succession, studied by Max Wertheimer and foundational to Gestalt psychology
Studied in facial recognition and perceptual organization research
Smallest detectable difference between two stimuli, central to psychophysics and the Weber-Fechner law
German psychologist who founded Gestalt psychology after studying the phi phenomenon
Neurological condition where stimulating one sense triggers an experience in another, such as seeing colors when hearing sounds
Sensory modality central to perceptual organization and illusion research
Perceptual process for identifying faces, studied via the fusiform face area and holistic processing
Studied in facial recognition and perceptual organization research
Sound-symbolism experiment where people match nonsense words to shapes, suggesting cross-modal perceptual associations
Gestalt law of "good form" holding that perception organizes ambiguous stimuli into the simplest, most orderly shape
German Gestalt psychologist known for insight-learning studies with chimpanzees

sections.name: Psychoanalysis & the Freudian Tradition
sections.blurb: Freud's theory of the unconscious and the analysts, concepts, and splinter schools it spawned.

entries.note: Austrian founder of psychoanalysis; theorized id/ego/superego, the Oedipus complex, and dream interpretation via free association
Austrian psychoanalyst who broke from Freud to found individual psychology, emphasizing the inferiority complex
Swiss psychiatrist who broke from Freud to found analytical psychology, theorizing archetypes and the collective unconscious
French psychoanalyst (b. 1901) known for the "return to Freud," the mirror stage, and structuralist reinterpretation of the unconscious
German-American neo-Freudian psychoanalyst who critiqued Freud's views on female psychology and theorized basic anxiety
Austrian-American psychoanalyst known for The Uses of Enchantment, a Freudian reading of fairy tales
Swiss psychiatrist who founded analytical psychology, theorizing archetypes, the collective unconscious, and introversion/extraversion
Austrian-British psychoanalyst who pioneered object relations theory and play therapy with children
Freud's therapeutic method and theory centered on unconscious conflict, dreams, and free association
Austrian psychoanalyst known for theories of orgone energy and character armor, later ostracized from mainstream psychoanalysis
Austrian-British psychoanalyst, Sigmund Freud's daughter, who developed child psychoanalysis and ego defense mechanism theory
Freud viewed as the "royal road to the unconscious," analyzed via manifest and latent content
Freud's rational mediating component of personality, balancing the id's drives and the superego's morals
Austrian psychoanalyst, early Freud associate, known for The Trauma of Birth and a later independent theory of will
Freud's 1899 work arguing that dreams are wish-fulfillments revealing unconscious desires
Subject of Bruno Bettelheim's Freudian analysis in The Uses of Enchantment
Central to Lacan's "mirror stage" theory of infant self-recognition and ego formation
Greek mythological figure who unknowingly killed his father and married his mother, source of Freud's Oedipus complex
Freud's theory that young boys unconsciously desire their mother and rival their father
Freudian defense mechanism channeling unacceptable impulses into socially acceptable activities
Freud's concept (das Unheimliche) describing the unsettling feeling produced by something familiar yet strange
Redirection of a patient's feelings about a significant figure onto the therapist during psychoanalysis

sections.name: Behaviorism & Learning
sections.blurb: Conditioning-based accounts of learning built on animal experiments, from Pavlov's heirs through Watson, Thorndike, Skinner, and Bandura.

entries.note: Canadian-American psychologist known for social learning theory, self-efficacy, and the Bobo doll experiment
American psychologist known for the law of effect and puzzle-box experiments with cats
American psychologist who founded behaviorism and conducted the Little Albert experiment on conditioned fear
Common experimental animal used in conditioning and maze-learning studies
General learning process encompassing classical (Pavlovian) and operant (Skinnerian) forms
Animal used in Pavlov's classical conditioning experiments on salivary reflexes
General process of behavior change through experience, central to behaviorist theory
Apparatus used in animal learning studies, notably Tolman's cognitive-map research with rats
School holding that psychology should study only observable behavior, founded by Watson and advanced by Skinner
American behaviorist known for operant conditioning, the Skinner box, and radical behaviorism
Learning process pairing a neutral stimulus with an unconditioned stimulus, pioneered by Pavlov
Bandura's theory that behavior is learned through observation and imitation of models
American behaviorist known for operant conditioning and the Skinner box
Albert Bandura's 1961 study showing children imitate adults' aggressive actions toward an inflatable doll
Learning through reinforcement and punishment, formalized by B.F. Skinner using the Skinner box

sections.name: Developmental Psychology
sections.blurb: Stage theories and experiments tracing cognitive, social, and moral growth from infancy through adolescence.

entries.note: Swiss psychologist known for stages of cognitive development (sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete/formal operational) and object permanence
American psychologist known for stages of moral development assessed through Heinz's dilemma
Common subject of developmental research on cognitive, social, and moral growth
Broad topic studied via Kohlberg's stages of moral development and Gilligan's ethics-of-care critique
Used in studies like the Clark doll experiments on children's racial self-perception
German-American psychologist known for eight stages of psychosocial development spanning the lifespan
American psychologist known for rhesus monkey studies on attachment using wire and cloth surrogate mothers
Used in Harlow's attachment experiments with wire and cloth surrogate mothers
Capacity to attribute mental states to others, tested via false-belief tasks in children
Common subjects of early studies on attachment, object permanence, and perception
Russian psychologist known for sociocultural theory, the zone of proximal development, and scaffolding
Used in Walter Mischel's Stanford marshmallow test on delayed gratification in children
Central figures in attachment research, including Harlow's surrogate-mother studies
Studied via attachment styles and Baumrind's authoritative/authoritarian/permissive parenting typology
Emotional bond between infant and caregiver, studied by Bowlby and Ainsworth's strange situation procedure
Ability to resist immediate reward for a later one, studied in Mischel's marshmallow test
Common subjects in attachment and parenting-style research
American psychologist known for the Stanford marshmallow test on delayed gratification
Bowlby and Ainsworth theory of infant-caregiver bonds; Strange Situation classifies secure, anxious, and avoidant attachment
American psychologist who critiqued Kohlberg's moral stages, proposing an ethic-of-care perspective in In a Different Voice
German-American psychologist known for eight stages of psychosocial development and the identity crisis concept
Shorthand for Erik Erikson, known for eight stages of psychosocial development
Moral dilemma about stealing a drug to save a dying spouse, used by Kohlberg to assess stages of moral reasoning
Research design following the same subjects over an extended period, common in developmental psychology
Piaget's concept of understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight, achieved in the sensorimotor stage
Piaget's first stage of cognitive development (birth to age two), marked by learning through senses and motor actions
Subjects of twin studies examining the heritability of intelligence, personality, and behavior

sections.name: Cognitive Psychology, Memory & Decision-Making
sections.blurb: Information-processing approaches to attention, memory, and judgment, including the heuristics-and-biases program.

entries.note: Broad topic covering encoding, storage, and retrieval processes studied by Ebbinghaus, Loftus, and others
Israeli-American Nobel laureate who developed prospect theory and the System 1/System 2 heuristics framework with Amos Tversky
Broad cognitive process studied via selective attention, inattentional blindness, and the Stroop effect
American cognitive psychologist known for research on the misinformation effect and implanting false memories
Short-term system for holding and manipulating information, modeled by Baddeley's multi-component model
Implicit memory effect where exposure to a stimulus influences response to a later stimulus
General term for mental-process approaches to psychology, as in cognitive psychology and cognitive therapy
Mental shortcuts for judgment studied by Kahneman and Tversky, including availability and representativeness
Brain structure central to memory formation and consolidation
Tendency to favor information confirming preexisting beliefs while discounting contradictory evidence
German psychologist who pioneered memory research using nonsense syllables and charted the forgetting curve
Heuristic bias where initial information disproportionately influences subsequent judgments
Systematic errors in judgment studied in Kahneman and Tversky's heuristics-and-biases program
Loss of memory over time, studied via Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve
Failure to notice an unexpected stimulus when attention is focused elsewhere, shown in the "invisible gorilla" study
Neurons that fire both when performing an action and observing it performed, linked to empathy and imitation
Studied via prospect theory's framing of risk-averse and risk-seeking choices under gains and losses
Temporary memory store of limited capacity, linked to Miller's "seven plus or minus two" finding
Canadian-American cognitive scientist known for works like The Blank Slate and The Language Instinct
Task measuring interference when naming the ink color of mismatched color words, demonstrating selective attention
Memory-loss disorder studied through cases like patient H.M. and distinctions between anterograde and retrograde types
Systematic deviation from rational judgment, catalogued extensively by Kahneman and Tversky
Long-term memory for specific personal events, distinguished from semantic memory by Endel Tulving
Vivid, detailed memory of the circumstances surrounding a highly emotional or surprising event
American economist-psychologist known for bounded rationality and the concept of "satisficing" in decision-making
Kahneman and Tversky's model of decision-making under risk, incorporating loss aversion and reference points
Measure of speed of response to a stimulus, used across cognitive and perceptual experiments
Likely refers to George Miller's "The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two" on working memory capacity
Interference effect where naming ink color is slowed when the word names a different color

sections.name: Social Psychology & Landmark Experiments
sections.blurb: Classic obedience, conformity, and group-behavior studies, plus the attribution and prejudice research they inspired.

entries.note: American social psychologist known for cognitive dissonance theory and the doomsday-cult study in When Prophecy Fails
American social psychologist known for obedience experiments with fake electric shocks administered to a "learner"
Generalized beliefs about social groups, studied via stereotype threat and implicit bias research
Festinger's theory of mental discomfort from holding contradictory beliefs, reduced through attitude change or rationalization
Tendency to overemphasize dispositional over situational factors when explaining others' behavior
Tendency to align behavior or beliefs with a group, studied in Asch's line-judgment experiments
Polish-American social psychologist known for conformity experiments using line-length judgment tasks
Muzafer Sherif's study of intergroup conflict and cooperation among boys at a summer camp
American university where Philip Zimbardo conducted the Stanford Prison Experiment simulating guards and prisoners
Process of explaining behavior's causes, studied via the fundamental attribution error and actor-observer bias
Cognitive bias where a positive impression of one trait influences perception of other, unrelated traits
Philip Zimbardo's 1971 study simulating guards and prisoners, showing situational power over behavior
Phenomenon where individuals are less likely to help a victim when other bystanders are present
Refers to the replication crisis, the difficulty of reproducing findings from classic psychology studies
Phenomenon where awareness of a negative stereotype impairs performance in the stereotyped domain
American psychologist who conducted the Stanford Prison Experiment on the situational power of assigned roles
Acronym for Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic, a critique of psychology's reliance on unrepresentative samples
Leon Festinger's book on a doomsday cult's reaction to a failed prophecy, illustrating cognitive dissonance
Studied via Bandura's Bobo doll experiment on observational learning of aggressive behavior
Fake shocks administered to a "learner" in Milgram's obedience experiments
Tendency to develop preference for a stimulus through repeated exposure, studied by Robert Zajonc
Turkish-American social psychologist known for the Robbers Cave Experiment on intergroup conflict
Negative attitude toward a social group, studied via stereotypes, in-group/out-group bias, and contact theory
Likely refers to Kenneth and Mamie Clark, whose doll experiments demonstrated internalized racial bias in Black children
Studied via deindividuation and group-behavior research such as Le Bon's crowd psychology
Measure of automatic, unconscious associations between concepts, often used to study implicit bias
American social psychologist known for moral foundations theory and The Righteous Mind
German-American psychologist considered a founder of social psychology, known for field theory and leadership-style studies
Tendency for individuals to exert less effort on a task when working in a group than alone
Ability to form accurate judgments from brief exposure to behavior, popularized by Malcolm Gladwell's Blink

sections.name: Personality & Psychological Testing
sections.blurb: Trait theories, intelligence testing, and the projective and self-report instruments used to measure individual differences.

entries.note: Broad topic covering trait, psychodynamic, and humanistic theories of stable individual differences
Cognitive capacity measured by IQ tests, theorized via Spearman's g factor and Gardner's multiple intelligences
One of the Big Five personality traits, reflecting emotional instability and anxiety proneness
Projective personality test using inkblots to assess unconscious thought patterns
Projective test using ambiguous scene cards to elicit stories revealing unconscious motives, developed by Henry Murray
Statistical method for identifying underlying traits, used by Spearman's g factor and Cattell's 16 factors
French psychologist who developed the first practical intelligence test with Theodore Simon
Tendency to accept vague, generic personality descriptions as uniquely accurate, exploited by horoscopes and the Forer study
British psychologist who developed factor analysis and theorized a general intelligence "g factor"
Likely refers to Gordon Allport, American psychologist known for trait theory of personality
Self-report personality inventory sorting people into 16 types based on Jungian typology
British-American psychologist who used factor analysis to identify 16 personality factors
Site of the development of the MMPI (Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory)

sections.name: Motivation, Emotion & Humanistic Psychology
sections.blurb: Maslow, Rogers, and the study of drives, feelings, and self-actualization as an alternative to behaviorism and psychoanalysis.

entries.note: General topic covering affective states and theories like the James-Lange and Cannon-Bard theories
American humanistic psychologist known for the hierarchy of needs culminating in self-actualization
Physiological and psychological state of alertness tied to the Yerkes-Dodson law of optimal performance
American humanistic psychologist who developed client-centered therapy and unconditional positive regard
American psychologist known for learned helplessness research and founding positive psychology
Emotional and motivational topic studied via Sternberg's triangular theory and attachment styles
Broad topic covering the drives behind behavior, from Maslow's hierarchy to intrinsic/extrinsic distinctions
Field founded by Martin Seligman studying human flourishing, strengths, and well-being
Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi's concept of complete absorption and engagement in an activity
Central subject of positive psychology research on well-being and life satisfaction
Studied as an emotional and cognitive response, explained via incongruity and relief theories
Austrian psychiatrist and Holocaust survivor who founded logotherapy, described in Man's Search for Meaning
General term for emotional experience and expression, studied alongside mood and feeling
Brain structure central to processing fear and other emotional responses
Cognitive capacity for novel idea generation, studied within humanistic and cognitive frameworks
Capacity to perceive, understand, and manage emotions, popularized by Daniel Goleman
Concept central to Rogers's self-concept and related identity theories
Institution where Martin Seligman founded the positive psychology movement

sections.name: Clinical, Abnormal & Health Psychology
sections.blurb: Mental disorders, their diagnostic classification, and the therapeutic approaches developed to treat them.

entries.note: Severe disorder marked by hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thought, historically linked to the dopamine hypothesis
Neurodevelopmental condition involving social communication difficulties and restricted, repetitive behaviors
Mood disorder of persistent sadness and loss of interest, studied via learned helplessness and treated with CBT
Neurodevelopmental condition on the autism spectrum involving social and communication differences
Seligman's finding that exposure to inescapable adverse events reduces later attempts to escape controllable ones
Post-traumatic stress disorder following trauma exposure, with symptoms including flashbacks and hyperarousal
Physiological and psychological response to demands, studied via Selye's general adaptation syndrome
Historically pathologized in early DSM editions; removed as a disorder classification in 1973
Personality trait or disorder involving grandiosity and need for admiration, named for the Greek myth figure
Anxiety-related disorder marked by intrusive thoughts and repetitive compulsive behaviors
Compulsive substance use or behavior despite harm, studied via reward pathways and dependence
Mood disorder alternating manic and depressive episodes
Therapy combining cognitive restructuring and behavioral techniques, pioneered by Aaron Beck and Albert Ellis
General term for talk-based treatment of mental disorders across many theoretical orientations
American psychologist who developed rational emotive behavior therapy, a precursor to cognitive behavioral therapy
Severe, persistent form of depression treated via psychotherapy and antidepressant medication
Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, the standard classification system used by American psychiatry
Disorder marked by grandiosity, need for admiration, and lack of empathy
Improvement in symptoms from an inert treatment due to patient expectation
Disorder following trauma exposure marked by flashbacks, avoidance, and hyperarousal
Psychological response to distressing events, central to PTSD and to treatments described in The Body Keeps the Score
American psychiatrist who developed cognitive therapy for depression, identifying automatic negative thoughts
Emotional state of worry or fear underlying anxiety disorders, studied across cognitive and biological frameworks
Disorder marked by unstable relationships, self-image, and emotions, often treated with dialectical behavior therapy
British psychologist known for autism research and the "mindblindness" theory-of-mind account of autism
Substance use disorder involving compulsive alcohol consumption despite negative consequences
Eating disorder marked by restricted eating and intense fear of weight gain
Neurodevelopmental disorder marked by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity
Fifth edition (2013) of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, the current U.S. diagnostic standard
Category including anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and binge eating disorder
Distress from incongruence between experienced gender and sex assigned at birth, classified in the DSM-5
Institutions for treating severe mental illness, subject of critiques by Thomas Szasz and studies like Rosenhan's "On Being Sane in Insane Places"

entries.topic: The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma
entries.answerline: The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma
entries.note: Bessel van der Kolk's book on how trauma is stored in the body and treated through somatic approaches
Hungarian-American psychiatrist known for critiquing mental illness as a social construct in The Myth of Mental Illness

appendix.answer: Man's Search for Meaning: An Introduction to Logotherapy
The Lucifer Effect: Understanding How Good People Turn Evil
